title: The Top 20 Performances of 2020 and 'Ma Rainey's Black Bottom'
format: New York, LA, and Boston hand out the season's first critics awards, Chadwick Boseman and Viola Davis anchor a review of Ma Rainey's Black Bottom, and Sean and Amanda draft dueling, unordered lists of the year's best film performances, plus two side awards for drunk dads and kid actors.

segments.heading: The critics circle awards
segments.note: New York, LA, and Boston kick off award season

films.title: First Cow
films.note: NYFCC's Best Picture. Sean reads off the circle's last decade of Best Picture picks: not one of them went on to win the Oscar.

films.title: Da 5 Bloods
films.note: NYFCC named Chadwick Boseman Best Supporting Actor for it; Delroy Lindo was also recognized.

films.title: Borat Subsequent Moviefilm
films.note: Maria Bakalova took NYFCC Best Supporting Actress.

films.title: Never Rarely Sometimes Always
films.note: Sidney Flanigan won NYFCC Best Actress, Eliza Hittman Best Screenplay.

films.title: Nomadland
films.note: Chloé Zhao won NYFCC Best Director and was LAFCA's Best Picture runner-up, behind Small Axe.

films.title: Mank
films.note: Amanda Seyfried was LAFCA's Best Supporting Actress runner-up; the film still hasn't broken into Netflix's top 10 the way Ma Rainey has.

films.title: Sound of Metal
films.note: Paul Raci was LAFCA's Best Supporting Actor runner-up behind Ma Rainey's Glynn Turman; Riz Ahmed was Best Actor runner-up behind Boseman.

films.title: Promising Young Woman
films.note: Carey Mulligan won LAFCA Best Actress, Emerald Fennell Best Screenplay.

films.title: Minari
films.note: Yuh-Jung Youn won LAFCA Best Supporting Actress.

films.title: Time
films.note: Garrett Bradley's documentary, both circles' best-documentary pick.

films.title: Wolfwalkers
films.note: Both circles' best-animation pick.

films.title: The Forty-Year-Old Version
films.note: Radha Blank's directorial debut, cited by both circles for the new-generation/first-time filmmaker award.

segments.heading: Ma Rainey's Black Bottom
segments.note: Netflix's adaptation of August Wilson's 1984 play

films.title: Ma Rainey's Black Bottom
films.note: George C. Wolfe directs the adaptation of Wilson's Pittsburgh Cycle play, which was No. 1 on Netflix's daily top 10 the morning they recorded. LAFCA named Glynn Turman Best Supporting Actor and Chadwick Boseman Best Actor for it; Viola Davis was the Best Actress runner-up. It's one of Boseman's final performances and an unusual one for him: not the institution he usually plays but, per Amanda, 'the firework as opposed to the fuse,' building to a single closing monologue. Davis, physically transformed as Rainey, doesn't get overshadowed. Also strong: Colman Domingo and Taylour Paige, on her way to Zola.

films.title: Sound of Metal
films.note: Riz Ahmed as a punk-metal drummer losing his hearing. Carries almost every frame of a movie that's found an unlikely cult following on streaming.

films.title: Mangrove
films.note: Shaun Parkes as Frank Crichlow, owner of the restaurant at the center of the Mangrove Nine story. He holds the first 40 minutes before Malachi Kirby and Letitia Wright take over.

films.title: Da 5 Bloods
films.note: Delroy Lindo as a Vietnam vet in a MAGA hat, coming apart at the seams. 'The engine of this movie' (big and specific at the same time).

films.title: Nomadland
films.note: Frances McDormand, defended against a friend's take that she's overrated: she has what every great movie star has, a persona, and it's always recognizably hers.

films.title: First Cow
films.note: Orion Lee, opposite John Magaro, as the half of the film's central friendship Sean can't take his eyes off.

films.title: Borat Subsequent Moviefilm
films.note: Maria Bakalova, 24 and previously unknown, matching Sacha Baron Cohen scene for scene, including the Rudy Giuliani sequence.

films.title: I'm Thinking of Ending Things
films.note: Jessie Buckley playing a woman who is, in fact, someone else's psyche (and, at one point, Pauline Kael).

films.title: Mank
films.note: Amanda Seyfried as Marion Davies, all surface charm until you notice what's underneath it.

films.title: Freaky
films.note: Vince Vaughn playing a body-swapped teenage girl in Christopher Landon's slasher comedy, a real return to his comic register.

films.title: The Trial of the Chicago 7
films.note: Yahya Abdul-Mateen II as Bobby Seale, set apart from the other seven and nailing Sorkin's rhythm.

films.title: The Assistant
films.note: Julia Garner, on screen nearly the entire film, as an assistant to a Weinstein-esque producer.

films.title: Pieces of a Woman
films.note: Vanessa Kirby, especially across an unbroken opening stretch Sean won't spoil.

films.title: On the Rocks
films.note: Special award, middle-aged men who drink too much (three-way tie): Bill Murray at full wattage as a father who can charm his way out of anything.

films.title: Another Round
films.note: Same special award: Mads Mikkelsen, cast against type, as an empathetic teacher who starts day-drinking with his friends. Sean singles out the last five minutes.

films.title: The Way Back
films.note: Same special award: Ben Affleck, unsubtle but committed, drinking his life apart. 'The patron saint of the show.'

films.title: The King of Staten Island
films.note: Honorable mention: Bill Burr and Marisa Tomei. Sean wishes the movie had just been about them, retitled The King and Queen of Staten Island.

films.title: Tenet
films.note: John David Washington and Robert Pattinson, both on Amanda's list. Washington reinvents the Nolan hero with an ease and humor she says the film needed to have fun; Pattinson is a charm machine doing his own Eames-via-Tom-Hardy routine.

films.title: Palm Springs
films.note: Cristin Milioti, matching and then outrunning Andy Samberg's comic register while also carrying the film's emotional weight.

films.title: Kajillionaire
films.note: Gina Rodriguez, playing charm and normalcy against Evan Rachel Wood's family of scammers: the first character in a Miranda July movie not doing Miranda July's usual tone.

films.title: Minari
films.note: Steven Yeun, doing a kind of restraint Amanda calls rare. Alan Kim, as his young son, wins the special kid-acting award.

films.title: Let Them All Talk
films.note: Candice Bergen, stealing a Steven Soderbergh cruise-ship movie as both the comic relief and the emotional gut-punch opposite Meryl Streep.

films.title: Happiest Season
films.note: Aubrey Plaza, whose Riley performance got the internet rooting for the wrong ending for two straight days.

films.title: News of the World
films.note: Helena Zengel, opposite Tom Hanks, wins the other half of the special kid-acting award. The movie only works because she and Hanks form a bond.

referenced.group: A year ago at this time
films: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood

referenced.group: NYFCC Best Picture, the last decade - no Oscar winners among them
